name: Quantum many-body dynamics
lede: We use attosecond science not as an electron-only stopwatch, but as a time-energy microscope for coupled quantum matter.
body: We study how electrons and holes, nuclei and phonons, spin, topology and collective fluctuations exchange energy and information while a material or molecule changes its state.

The approach combines coherent attosecond soft-X-ray spectroscopy with new observables for fluctuations and correlations, so that both ordered motion and incoherent many-body dynamics become measurable.
The aim is a more complete experimental view of quantum dynamics — from the first femtoseconds of excitation to the emergence of phases, functionality, entropy production and dissipation.

name: Quantum materials and phase transitions
lede: Functionality in quantum matter often appears when degrees of freedom become inseparable. We use ultrafast and strong-field probes to follow this reorganization in real time.
body: We investigate superconductors, charge-density-wave systems, bad metals, topological and valley materials, and other correlated phases where conventional quasiparticle pictures can fail.

High-harmonic spectroscopy provides a coherent strong-field view of material phases, while photon-statistical measurements add sensitivity to fluctuations, correlations and entropy-like information that ordinary intensity measurements miss.
The long-term goal is to connect microscopic non-equilibrium dynamics to macroscopic material properties: conductivity, topology, phase stability, energy flow and emergent order.

name: Molecular quantum dynamics and imaging
lede: Chemical change is a many-body process: charge rearrangement, nuclear motion and non-adiabatic coupling unfold together. We image these processes in time and, where possible, in real space.
body: Attosecond core-level spectroscopy gives element-specific access to evolving electronic structure and to the nuclear motion encoded in transient spectral shifts and line shapes.
Single-electron quantum microscopy and reaction-microscope techniques complement this by resolving correlated electron-ion motion and structural change during bond breaking.
We use these methods to study non-adiabatic dynamics, conical intersections, molecular isomerization and the microscopic pathways by which light is converted into chemical motion.

name: Light-field technology and nonlinear optics
lede: The questions are only reachable because we build the sources ourselves: few-cycle lasers, OPCPA systems, waveform-controlled fields, high harmonics and attosecond soft-X-ray pulses.
body: Our group pioneered CEP-preserving OPCPA and long-wavelength strong-field scaling, enabling attosecond spectroscopy, electron diffraction, high-harmonic spectroscopy and coherent soft-X-ray generation.
Current technology spans the UV to the mid-IR and far-IR: few-cycle and single-cycle sources, field-resolved coherent supercontinua, high-repetition-rate drivers and table-top soft-X-ray beamlines.

The 19.2 attosecond pulse record is one milestone in this technological lineage, but the broader purpose is to create the tools needed for the next generation of many-body and molecular dynamics experiments.
